component_identification.content: Official base set components: 84 land tiles total (including 1 dark-backed start tile and 12 river tiles used with river rules), a scoreboard, and 40 regular meeples (8 in each of five colors). The modern base box also includes 5 abbots (1 per color), though first-game/base teaching commonly sets abbots aside. For standard base play, each player starts with 7 meeples in supply and places one extra meeple of their color on 0 of the scoring track as a score marker.

core_game_loop.content: Each turn has three ordered steps: place one drawn tile, optionally place one meeple from your supply on that just-placed tile, then score any features completed by the placement. Tile edges must match adjacent terrain types (city to city, road to road, field to field). Meeples claim features as knights, highwaymen, monks (and farmers in full rules), and remain committed until those features score (farmers typically stay to endgame). After scoring, recovered meeples return to supply for reuse on later turns.

detailed_rules.content:
Tile placement is mandatory: draw one tile and place it legally connected to at least one existing tile, matching all touching edges. If a drawn tile has no legal placement, remove it from play and draw next (rare). After placing a tile, you may place exactly one meeple from your supply on one feature of that tile—or place none.

Occupancy rule: you may place on a road/city/field/monastery only if no meeple already occupies that connected feature (consider full connected area, not just local tile segment). Later tile placements can merge features, creating shared control conflicts that are resolved by majority at scoring.

Road scoring: a road completes when both ends are closed by city/village/monastery junctions or loop closure. Completed roads score 1 point per tile in the road. City scoring during play: completed city scores 2 points per tile plus 2 per pennant/coats of arms. Monastery scoring during play: monastery is complete when surrounded by 8 adjacent tiles; it scores 9 points total (monastery tile + 8 neighbors).

Majority/ties: if multiple players have equal most meeples in a completed feature, all tied players receive full points (points are not split). After scoring roads/cities/monasteries, involved meeples return to owners.

Fields/farmers: farmers are placed lying down on field areas and stay until game end; they do not return during normal turn scoring. Field connectivity is separated by city walls/roads according to field boundaries.

Endgame and incomplete features: when final tile is placed, perform final scoring for incomplete roads, cities, monasteries, and fields. Incomplete roads/cities score reduced values compared with completed-city scoring in-game (roads still 1 per tile; incomplete cities 1 per tile and 1 per pennant). Incomplete monasteries score 1 point for monastery tile plus each surrounding tile present.

scoring_and_endgame.content: During the game, completed roads, cities, and monasteries score immediately for their controller(s). At game end (after last tile), unfinished roads and monasteries still score based on tiles present, and unfinished cities score at reduced rate (1 per tile + 1 per pennant). Field scoring is then resolved: each field scores 3 points for each completed city that field touches, awarded to player(s) with majority farmers in that field; ties grant full points to all tied majorities. The player with highest total score wins. If tied, tied players share victory.

beginner_strategy.content: Keep your meeple economy healthy: early points are great, but running out of available meeples removes your tactical flexibility. Favor short roads and medium cities you can realistically close, then recycle those meeples quickly. Use placement to influence opponents as much as yourself—feeding a tile that completes their large city is often worse than taking a smaller personal gain elsewhere. Learn when to invest in farms: one good farm touching multiple likely-completed cities can outscore several small features, but overcommitting farmers too early can starve your turn-by-turn scoring. Watch merge opportunities—joining your claimed feature to an opponent’s for a majority swing is one of the strongest tactical plays in base Carcassonne.

metadata.notes: Rewritten using official rulebook text in /content/rulebook-text as primary source.
